text: The surgeon observes a slightly fatty liver with adhesions between the left lobe and the phobic string. Adhesiolysis is performed to inspect the position and functionality of the phobic string.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to hold a small band of adhesion, with the lock of the ring visible on the right side in blue and the entire ring in white. The harmonic scalpel in the right hand gently cuts the adhesion to free the liver from the ring.

The surgeon attempts to insert a gastric calibration tube but is obstructed by the fauvist ring. They proceed to free the omentum from the lesser curvature of the stomach, approximately twelve centimeters below the fauvist ring, using a harmonic device. The omentum is carefully separated to create a retro gastric tunnel, forming a visible window.

The surgeon assesses the window created in the lesser curvature, ensuring it is sufficient for using the endo-GI linear stapler to divide the stomach. The greater curvature is visible, showing a sleeve stomach configuration. The plan involves creating a gastric pouch and performing a side-to-side anastomosis using the endo-GI linear stapler, focusing on the bypass component due to the existing phobias ring providing restriction.

The surgeon creates a retro-gastric tunnel by carefully lifting the stomach above the pancreas using the left hand, ensuring no injury to the omental bursa or pancreas. The harmonic device is used to separate the stomach, with its silicon jaw kept close to the stomach to prevent accidental perforation. The tunnel formation progresses slowly, with minor bleeding being coagulated during the process.

The surgeon completes the formation of the retro-gastric tunnel, with the window now visible. The liver is seen through the tunnel, and the window is enlarged to accommodate the endo GI stapler. The grasper is positioned over the anterior surface of the left lobe of the liver, indicating the left side of the stomach will be left intact while the right side is prepared for the gastrogenous stomach.

The surgeon uses an Endo-GIA linear stapler with a blue cartridge to divide the dilated portion of the stomach. The tip of the stapler is visible as it fires, creating a gastric pouch. Due to the sleeve gastrectomy, an additional cartridge is required to complete the division, resulting in the formation of the gastric pouch.

The surgeon brings the jejunostomy intestinal loop into view, checking the length of the gastric pouch, which is intentionally kept long due to the presence of the fauvist ring for better complication management.

The surgeon identifies the secondary gastric pedicle and lifts the omentum towards the transverse colon. The transverse colon is visible as the omentum is elevated. The surgeon then pulls down the small intestine to locate the beginning of the jejunum, identifying anatomical landmarks in the process.

The surgeon measures the jejunum, counting increments of five centimeters to reach a total of 250 centimeters for the malabsorptive procedure. The jejunum is carefully spread to avoid torsion. An anastomosis is planned at this point, with an 8mm opening created in both the gastric pouch and the small intestine using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon ensures the mucosa is visible while perforating the stomach, using graspers to enter the lumen. The lumen of both the intestine and stomach are clearly visible, facilitating the creation of an anastomosis.

The surgeon creates a small perforation in the anti-mesenteric border of the intestine and enlarges it using the vibrating jaw of the harmonic device. The incision is carefully widened with anterior traction to avoid contact with the opposite mucosa. The lumen is confirmed by introducing a grasper. The endo-GIL linear stapler is prepared, with the face jaw positioned in the intestine and the cartridge jaw in the stomach, aligned parallel for controlled firing. This setup facilitates the mini gastric bypass with an end-to-side anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the side-to-side anastomosis between the jejunum and the gastric pouch. The internal suture line is inspected, showing no signs of bleeding. The mucosa of both the jejunum and stomach is clearly visible. Suturing is initiated to secure the anastomosis.

The surgeon sutures the corners of the anastomosis using a bi-directional barb suture with a pre-tied loop. The suture is applied in a full thickness, single layer technique, moving from out to in and then in to out. The barb suture locks itself without the need for a starter knot, allowing for continuous suturing and tightening with the grasper.

The surgeon begins suturing the full thickness of the gastric pouch, including the mucosa, ensuring an airtight closure. Methylene blue dye leak test is planned using 150 ml due to the large size of the gastric pouch. The jejunum and intestine are clamped with atraumatic graspers. The closure progresses layer by layer, using a two-third curve needle with a barb suture, while avoiding traction over the needle. The lesser omentum is visible attached to the gastric loop, providing support during closure.

The surgeon uses a barb suture with laser cuts every one millimeter to secure the tissue during the final stage of the mini gastric bypass closure. The suture tightens automatically upon pulling, providing enhanced tissue grip compared to normal vicryl. Despite the self-locking nature of the barb suture, a termination knot is applied for added security.

The surgeon uses two needle holders and graspers to tighten the sutures, ensuring they do not override each other. The open jaw of the grasper is used to pull and support the sutures, making them tighter. The needle holder is effective for holding the needle but can be traumatic to the tissue, so the grasper is preferred for handling within the lumen. The sutures are gradually tightened and closed within the lumen.

The surgeon reinforces the suture line with additional bites to ensure there is no chance of leakage. The needle is passed through the loop to lock the suture, followed by a termination bite to secure the closure.

The surgeon tightens the barb suture by pulling with a needle holder and pressing the tissue with an open jaw, ensuring the suture is locked. A square knot is used to terminate the suture on healthy tissue, avoiding the intra-atomic side. The needle is left in place, and the loop is tightened as in open surgery, completing the termination with another half knot.

The surgeon cuts and removes the needle, then performs a leak test by injecting 150 mL of methylene blue dye. An atraumatic intestinal clamp holds the afferent and efferent loops. The dye distends the area, and the surgeon checks the anastomosis and suturing site for leaks. No leaks are observed, and the area remains distended.

The surgeon checks for leaks and bleeding after completing the mini gastric bypass procedure. The surgical site is observed to be collapsed after releasing the grasper, and the phobias ring is visible.
